source: BCT标准教程 第2册 (Business Chinese Test Standard Course Book 2), People's Education Press, 2015. Lessons 1–11 (of 20). Level: BCT Level 2 / approx. HSK 2–3.
note_for_learner: This chunk covers Lessons 1–11 of a beginner-intermediate business Chinese textbook. Content is elementary for an HSK 5–6 reader, but the business register formulas, comparison grammar, and functional message templates are directly reusable in professional contexts.

model_essays.title: 商务访问日程介绍 (Presenting a Business Visitor's Itinerary)
model_essays.genre: Spoken itinerary briefing / business hospitality script
model_essays.text: 李先生，我来介绍一下您在中国的日程。您的飞机是11月15号十点到，我会去机场接您。我给您订了房间，我先送您去宾馆。中午钱经理请您吃饭，下午在我们公司开会。11月16号您可以休息一下，如果您想去走走，我可以送您去。您的飞机是11月17号早上十一点的，我早上八点去接您，送您去机场。您看这样的日程可以吗？如果您有什么需要，请告诉我，别客气。
model_essays.summary: A company liaison briefs an incoming business visitor on a three-day itinerary: airport pickup, hotel check-in, hosted lunch, afternoon meeting, free day, and departure send-off. Ends with an open offer to accommodate any needs.
model_essays.why_study: This is the most business-rich passage in the chunk. It models the standard formula for presenting a 接待 (reception/hospitality) itinerary in spoken Chinese — airport pickup, hosted meal, meeting, departure. The closing formula 如果您有什么需要，请告诉我，别客气 is a set phrase used universally in Chinese business hospitality. The text also densely illustrates 先...再/然后 sequencing and the polite request marker 您看...可以吗？
key_devices: 先...然后...再 sequencing for step-by-step agenda
如果您有什么需要，请告诉我，别客气 — standard hospitality closing
您看...可以吗？ — polite confirmation-seeking tag
给您订了 (booked for you), 请您吃饭 (treat you to a meal), 接您/送您 (pick you up/take you) — core reception collocations
Implicit politeness through all-second-person framing: every sentence centers the visitor's comfort

model_essays.title: 请假留言 (Sick-Leave Voicemail to Manager)
model_essays.genre: Business voice message / informal written memo
model_essays.text: 您好，李经理。我是CTI公司的李好。我生病了，要在家休息一下。下周一不能和您开会了。我星期五上班，我们可以星期五开会吗？请给我回个电话，谢谢。
model_essays.summary: An employee leaves a phone message for her manager: she is ill, cannot attend Monday's meeting, proposes rescheduling to Friday, and asks for a call-back.
model_essays.why_study: A compact, complete model of the Chinese business message formula: [greeting + self-identification] → [reason/situation] → [impact on plans] → [proposed alternative] → [action request] → [thanks]. This five-beat structure transfers directly to email and SMS in professional Chinese.
key_devices: Self-ID formula: 我是[公司]的[姓名] — always name your company before your name in formal messages
下周一不能和您...了 — 了 here signals a changed state (plan no longer holds)
请给我回个电话 — standard call-back request phrase
Compact closure: 谢谢 alone (no name) — appropriate for voice message

model_essays.title: 口头请假申请 (Verbal Sick-Leave Request to Manager)
model_essays.text: 李经理，您好，我是王欢。我生病了，今天去看了医生。医生说我感冒了，有点儿发烧，让我多休息。我想在家休息两天，可以吗？谢谢您。
model_essays.summary: An employee calls her manager to report illness: she saw a doctor, was diagnosed with a cold and mild fever, and requests two days of rest at home.
model_essays.why_study: Models how to frame a leave request in Chinese: lead with the medical fact (doctor's authority), then make the request, then seek approval with 可以吗？. The structure 医生说...让我...（做某事）is a polite way to back a request with professional authority rather than personal preference.
key_devices: 医生说...让我多休息 — cite authority (doctor's orders) to legitimize a request
有点儿发烧 — 有点儿 for mild, sympathy-evoking complaints
我想...，可以吗？ — soft permission-seeking pattern
谢谢您 (not 谢谢你) — 您 maintains formal register even with one's own manager

model_essays.title: 今天出了很多错 (Narrative: A Day Full of Mistakes)
model_essays.text: 今天我做错了很多事情。高飞让我买咖啡，我买错了，买了茶。李好让我三点开会，我听错了，两点去的，等了一个小时。王欢让我告诉她王经理的电话，我说错了，说的是李经理的电话。我这是怎么了？
model_essays.summary: A self-deprecating monologue: the narrator made three successive errors (bought the wrong drink, went to a meeting at the wrong time, gave the wrong phone number), ending with a rhetorical question of self-bewilderment.
model_essays.why_study: Excellent drill text for V+错 resultative complement — the single most useful pattern for describing workplace errors and corrections. The text also shows the 让 causative in parallel structure and the 是...的 focus construction (两点去的). The closing rhetorical 我这是怎么了？ is a natural idiomatic expression of exasperation.
key_devices: V+错 repeated in parallel: 买错了 / 听错了 / 说错了 — resultative complement pattern
让 causative: [person] 让我 [V] — reporting what you were asked to do
是...的 focus: 两点去的 — stresses the time (unexpected detail)
Anaphoric structure: each sentence follows the same shape, creating rhythm and cumulative comic effect
我这是怎么了？ — rhetorical self-questioning idiom

model_essays.title: 上班路途比较 (Comparing Commutes to Work)
model_essays.text: 你们每天怎么去上班？李好的家离公司很近，她每天走路上班，要走十分钟。我家离公司很远，我坐地铁上班，要坐十站。地铁虽然很快，但是人太多了。李经理的家也很远，他开车上班，要开一个小时。张迎的家不太远，她每天坐公共汽车上班，要坐半个小时。
model_essays.summary: A narrator describes how four people commute to work, contrasting distance, mode of transport, and time taken.
model_essays.why_study: Clean model of 离 (distance from), 要 (takes/costs time), and 虽然...但是... in a natural descriptive context. The passage shows how to write a short comparative paragraph about multiple people using parallel structure — useful for business reports comparing teams, options, or scenarios.
key_devices: 家离公司 + [近/远] — distance-from construction with 离
要 + [time/stations] — expressing duration or cost
虽然...但是... — concessive structure with real-world consequence
Parallel person-by-person structure: useful template for comparative business writing

model_essays.title: 怎么去北京医院 (Giving Directions to the Beijing Hospital)
model_essays.text: 怎么去北京医院？可以坐5路公共汽车，在北京医院站下车就行了，要坐一个多小时。公共汽车站很近，往东走一百多米，左转就到了。你也可以坐地铁，坐地铁比坐公共汽车快，在北京站下车，半个多小时就到了。地铁站要一直往西走，十多分钟就到了。
model_essays.summary: A speaker explains two routes to Beijing Hospital — by bus and by subway — including walking directions to each stop and estimated travel time.
model_essays.why_study: A reusable template for giving multi-option directions in Chinese. The passage models the 往 + direction + 走 + distance + turn + 就到了 template, the comparison 比...快, and the quantifier 多 (over/approximately) attached to distances and times. Professional contexts (meeting someone, explaining venue location) frequently require exactly this type of multi-option routing explanation.
key_devices: 往[direction]走[distance]，[turn]，就到了 — complete directions template
一百多米 / 半个多小时 — 多 as approximator (just over / more than)
坐地铁比坐公共汽车快 — direct comparison with 比
就 — immediacy marker (right away, as soon as you...)
一直 往西走 — 一直 for straight-ahead navigation

grammar_patterns.explanation: 着 attached to a verb indicates a state resulting from an action that is still in progress (not the action itself). Commonly used to describe what people are wearing or how they are positioned. Distinguished from 在/正在 which describe an active ongoing action.
examples: 站着的那位 (the one standing [over there])
他穿着西装，打着领带 (he is wearing a suit and tie)
戴着白色的眼镜 (wearing white glasses)

grammar_patterns.explanation: Standard concessive conjunction pair. 虽然 introduces the conceded fact; 但是 or 可是 introduces the contrasting reality. In informal speech 虽然 is often dropped, leaving just 但是. Elevated register: 尽管...但是...
examples: 虽然工作有点儿忙，但是我很喜欢这个工作。
地铁虽然很快，但是人太多了。

grammar_patterns.explanation: 过 after a verb marks that an experience has been had at some unspecified past time. Negated with 没(有)V过. Contrasts with 了 which marks completion of a specific past event. Key for talking about travel, education, and life experience.
examples: 我去过美国和日本。(I have been to the US and Japan.)
你学过汉语吗？(Have you ever studied Chinese?)
我吃过中国菜。(I have eaten Chinese food before.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 是...的 (focus/cleft construction for past events)
grammar_patterns.explanation: Used to focus attention on how, when, where, or with whom a past action took place — not whether it happened. The action is presupposed; 是...的 spotlights the circumstance. Extremely common in business Chinese to clarify details.
examples: 我今天是走路来上班的。(I walked to work today — emphasizing mode of transport)
两点去的。(I went at two — emphasizing the unexpected time)
是chuānzhe hēisè kùzi的那位吗？(Is it the one wearing black trousers?)

grammar_patterns.explanation: 有点儿 precedes adjectives to express a mild degree, typically with a slight negative or complaint nuance. Contrasts with 挺...的 (quite — positive) and 非常 (very — neutral/strong). Use 有点儿 when the speaker perceives the degree as inconvenient or unfortunate.
examples: 我有点儿累。(I'm a bit tired.)
工作有点儿忙。(Work is a bit hectic.)
有点儿发烧。(A slight fever.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 挺 + adj + 的 (quite/pretty — colloquial positive)
grammar_patterns.explanation: 挺...的 expresses a fairly high but not extreme degree, usually with positive or neutral connotation. The final 的 is characteristic of this construction and softens/personalizes the statement. More casual than 非常; warmer than just 很.
examples: 我挺喜欢这个工作的。(I quite like this job.)
这儿挺舒服的。(It's quite comfortable here.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 每 + [time unit] + 都 + V (every [unit], all/always V)
grammar_patterns.explanation: 每 (every/each) pairs with 都 (all) to express habitual or universal action. The 都 is nearly obligatory and cannot usually be omitted. Extended: 什么...都... (everything...) and 哪儿...都... (everywhere...).
examples: 我每天都去跑步。(I run every day.)
我什么菜都喜欢。(I like all kinds of food.)
她每天上班前和下班后都去跑步。(She runs both before and after work every day.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: A 比 B + adj (A is more [adj] than B)
grammar_patterns.explanation: Standard comparison structure. The adjective stands alone — no 更 is needed (though 更 can be added for emphasis). To say 'A is much more X than B': A 比 B + adj + 多了/得多. Negated: A 没有 B + adj (A is not as X as B).
examples: 昨天比今天热。(Yesterday was hotter than today.)
坐地铁比坐公共汽车快。(The subway is faster than the bus.)
北京下雨的天气温度比晴天低。(Beijing's rainy-day temperature is lower than sunny days.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: A 没有 B + adj (A is not as [adj] as B)
grammar_patterns.explanation: Negative comparison. A 没有 B + adj means 'A does not reach B's level of [adj]'. This is the negative form of the 比 construction and is interchangeable in meaning with B 比 A + adj. Often sounds more natural in spoken Chinese when emphasizing that something falls short.
examples: 温度没有今天高。(The temperature won't be as high as today's.)
今天没有昨天热。(Today is not as hot as yesterday.)

grammar_patterns.explanation: 要 as a predicate expressing the cost or duration required. Extremely common for discussing travel time, process time, and quantities. Distinct from modal 要 (want to / will).
examples: 坐公共汽车要多长时间？(How long does the bus take?)
走路要十分钟。(It takes ten minutes on foot.)
要坐五站。(Five stops to go.)

grammar_patterns.explanation: Sequence-of-steps structure. 先 (first), 然后 (then/after that), 再 (then again/next). Use for ordered instructions, itineraries, and procedures. 先 is typically tied to the subject; 然后/再 can start new clauses or sentences. Essential for business planning language.
examples: 我先去看医生，然后再上班。(I'll go to the doctor first, then come to work.)
我先送您去宾馆，中午请您吃饭，下午开会。(I'll take you to the hotel first, then lunch at noon, then a meeting in the afternoon.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 正在 / 在 + V (progressive: currently doing)
grammar_patterns.explanation: 正在 (or just 在) before a verb marks an ongoing action at the moment of reference. Often paired with 呢 at the end. In business calls, 正在开会 is the standard phrase for 'in a meeting'. 刚才 + 在 + V signals an action that was in progress at a slightly earlier moment.
examples: 他正在开会。(He is currently in a meeting.)
刚才我在开会。(I was in a meeting just now.)
李小姐正在唱歌。(Miss Li is singing.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 让 causative: [A] 让 [B] V (A asks/makes/lets B do V)
grammar_patterns.explanation: 让 expresses permission, instruction, or causation. Reading depends on context: let (permission), ask/tell (instruction), make (causation). In workplace Chinese, the pattern is extremely common for reporting instructions received or given. Similar to 叫 but more standard.
examples: 医生让我多休息。(The doctor told me to get more rest.)
高飞让我买咖啡。(Gao Fei asked me to buy coffee.)
李经理让我来接您。(Manager Li asked me to come and pick you up.)

grammar_patterns.explanation: Conditional polite request structure commonly used in business Chinese to make open-ended offers of assistance. The 如果 clause sets the condition; 请 + V delivers the polite instruction. This pattern is a standard closing formula in Chinese business correspondence and speech.
examples: 如果您有什么需要，请告诉我。(If there is anything you need, please let me know.)
如果您想去走走，我可以送您去。(If you'd like to take a walk, I can take you.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 快 + [adj/V] + 了 (about to / on the verge of)
grammar_patterns.explanation: 快...了 expresses imminent change of state — something is about to happen or a situation is approaching its endpoint. Distinguished from 要...了 which can also be used, but 快...了 implies greater immediacy and is slightly more colloquial.
examples: 我快好了。(I'm almost better now.)
飞机快到了。(The plane is about to arrive.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: V + 错 (resultative complement: did V wrongly)
grammar_patterns.explanation: 错 as a resultative complement indicates the action produced an erroneous result. Covers a wide range: mishearing (听错), misspeaking (说错), miswriting (写错), misbuying (买错), misdialing (打错). Essential for professional apology and correction language.
examples: 对不起，我写错了。(Sorry, I wrote it down wrong.)
我听错了，我的意思是… (I misheard; what I meant was…)
我买错了，买了茶。(I bought the wrong thing — I got tea instead.)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 往/向 + direction + 走 + distance，然后 [turn]，就到了
grammar_patterns.explanation: Standard Chinese template for giving walking directions. 往/向 (toward) + compass direction + 走 (walk) + distance, followed by turning instruction, concluded with 就到了 (and you'll be there). 就 signals immediate arrival upon completion of the action.
examples: 您往东走三百米，就到了。(Walk three hundred meters eastward and you'll be there.)
往西走两百米，向左转，就到了。(Walk two hundred meters west, turn left, and you're there.)
一直往西走，十多分钟就到了。(Walk straight west and you'll be there in about ten minutes.)

expressions.meaning: to go on a business trip
to hold / attend a meeting
schedule; itinerary
message (n.); to leave a message (v.)
currently in a meeting (standard phone deflection phrase)

expressions.pinyin: qǐng gěi wǒ huí gè diànhuà
expressions.meaning: please call me back

expressions.pinyin: bù néng hé nín jiànmiàn / kāihuì
expressions.meaning: unable to meet with you / unable to hold the meeting (formal regret formula)
booked a room for you (hospitality phrase)
to treat you to a meal; to host you for lunch/dinner
to go to the airport to pick you up
to take/drive you to [place]; to see you off to
to complete formalities; to check in (hotel/office)

expressions.zh: 如果您有什么需要，请告诉我，别客气
expressions.pinyin: rúguǒ nín yǒu shénme xūyào, qǐng gàosu wǒ, bié kèqi
expressions.meaning: If there is anything you need, please don't hesitate to let me know (standard hospitality closing)
don't be polite / you're welcome / please don't stand on ceremony
What do you think of…? Does that work for you? (polite confirmation-seeking tag)
business center (building)
business room (hotel room class)
I truly appreciate it; I'm really grateful (warm, emphatic thanks)
thank you very much; I greatly appreciate it (formal thanks)
I'm truly sorry (stronger, more formal than 不好意思)
sorry / I'm embarrassed / excuse me (mild, versatile apology for minor inconvenience)
excuse me (when asking a favor or asking someone to move); more formal than 请问
no problem; never mind; it doesn't matter

expressions.zh: 对不起，我说错了，我的意思是…
expressions.pinyin: duìbuqǐ, wǒ shuō cuò le, wǒ de yìsi shì…
expressions.meaning: I'm sorry, I misspoke; what I meant was… (self-correction formula)
I am [Name] from [Company] — standard self-identification formula for phone calls and messages
not feeling well; feeling unwell (softer than 生病; common in workplace leave requests)
you should get more rest (standard caring advice / doctor's instruction formula)
long time no see (set greeting for reconnecting with acquaintances)
I see / I've got it / understood (acknowledging receipt of information; 了 marks newly acquired knowledge)
commerce/business (general, adj.: 商业中心 commerce center) vs. business affairs/commercial (formal: 商务签证 business visa, 商务舱 business class)
expressions.register: business standard — note register distinction between the two

expressions.meaning: What happened? / What's wrong? / How come? (inquiring about a changed state)
after work; after clocking off
straight ahead; all along; continuously
and you'll be right there; and you'll arrive immediately (direction closure phrase)
recently; lately (time reference for recent period, not 'nearest location')

essay_takeaways: BUSINESS MESSAGE FORMULA: Chinese professional messages follow a tight five-beat structure — [greeting + company + name] → [situation/reason] → [impact on plans] → [proposed alternative or request] → [polite close + thanks]. Apply this to emails, WeChat messages, and voice messages alike.

CITE AUTHORITY TO SOFTEN REQUESTS: Rather than saying 我想休息 (I want to rest), say 医生让我多休息 (the doctor told me to rest more). Citing a third-party authority — a doctor, a policy, a superior — makes a request feel less personal and more professionally grounded in Chinese business culture.

HOSPITALITY CLOSING FORMULA: 如果您有什么需要，请告诉我，别客气 is a stock phrase that closes nearly every Chinese business welcome/reception context. Memorize and deploy it whenever hosting a visitor, ending a meeting arrangement, or closing a service interaction.

APOLOGY REGISTER LADDER: 不好意思 (mild — for small awkwardnesses) < 对不起 (standard apology) < 真抱歉 (sincere, formal regret) < 非常抱歉 (strong formal apology). Match the level to the severity of the situation. In business, overusing 对不起 can sound either sycophantic or alarmist; 真抱歉 or 非常抱歉 is appropriate for failing to keep a commitment.

PHONE DEFLECTION: 他正在开会 (he's in a meeting) is the single most common reason given in Chinese for why someone can't come to the phone. The follow-up pattern is: 要给他留言吗？/ 您要留言吗？(Would you like to leave a message?). Knowing this sequence from both sides is essential for business phone culture.

SELF-CORRECTION MID-SPEECH: 对不起，我说错了，我的意思是… is the clean formula for correcting a verbal error in Chinese. The V+错 resultative complement (听错/说错/写错/买错) covers the full range of common mistake types and is indispensable for graceful self-correction in meetings and calls.

COMPARISON STRUCTURES — TWO PATTERNS FOR ONE IDEA: Chinese has two standard comparison patterns. Use 比 (A 比 B + adj) to assert A exceeds B; use 没有 (A 没有 B + adj) to assert A falls short of B. Both appear constantly in business Chinese (comparing prices, schedules, speeds, options). Neither uses 更 in the basic form.

SEQUENCE LANGUAGE FOR AGENDAS AND PROCEDURES: 先...然后...再... is the backbone of presenting itineraries, instructions, and processes. It maps directly onto numbered steps in English (first / then / then). Professional contexts — presenting a visit schedule, explaining a procedure, walking through an agenda — all require fluent use of this chain.

DIRECTIONS TEMPLATE: Chinese directions follow a rigid template: 往/向[方向]走[距离]，然后[左/右]转，就到了. Knowing the four cardinal directions (东南西北) and terms 左转/右转/一直走 makes it possible to give or receive any navigation instruction in Chinese.

REGISTER NOTE — 您 VS. 你: Even with one's own manager, the textbook consistently uses 您 in formal/phone contexts. Chinese business culture maintains the 您 register in written messages, phone calls, and first-time meetings, even among colleagues with an established relationship. Reserve 你 for peer-level informal speech.
